fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon A\to B be a section of some r\colon B\to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f\colon A\to B is a retract of the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B}, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By , it suffices to show that the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B} is a proposition, which follows via and .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coherence of different notions of equivalence

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon A\to B be a section of some r\colon B\to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f\colon A\to B is a retract of the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B}, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By , it suffices to show that the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B} is a proposition, which follows via and .
Let f\colon A\to B be a retraction of some s\colon B\to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of -\circ f at 1_{A}.
The type of biinverses of a given function f\colon A\to B is a proposition.
Sections of sections and retractions of retractions are unique.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a function f\colon A\to B and a section g\colon B\to A witnessed by \epsilon \colon f\circ g = 1_{B}, we define the coherent remainder of \mathopen {}\left ({g,\epsilon }\right )\mathclose {} to be the following sum:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({g,\epsilon }\right )\mathclose {} :\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({\eta : g\circ f \sim 1_{A}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({\eta : g\circ f \sim 1_{A}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({\eta : g\circ f \sim 1_{A}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({\eta : g\circ f \sim 1_{A}}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For any f\colon A\to B, it is evident that the type of inverses to f is (up to reassociation) precisely the dependent sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathsf {section}\,f}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathsf {section}\,f}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathsf {section}\,f}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathsf {section}\,f}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coherent remainder as a path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon A\to B be a function equipped with a section \sigma \equiv \mathopen {}\left ({g\colon B\to A,\epsilon \colon f\circ g = 1_{B}}\right )\mathclose {}. We can equip the coherent remainder of \sigma with the structure of a path object as follows:

fr:ol.fr:li: We equip g\circ f\sim 1_{A} with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({ g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} = x }\right )\mathclose {}

For each \eta \colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A}, we likewise equip \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({ \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({\eta x}\right )\mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}

fr:li.fr:p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0,\eta _1\colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} and \eta _{01}\colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^*\colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _1\sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _0\sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma :\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({ \eta : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}=x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({ \eta : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}=x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({ \eta : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}=x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({ \eta : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}=x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({ \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({\eta x}\right )\mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given elements \mathopen {}\left ({\eta _0,\chi _0}\right )\mathclose {} and \mathopen {}\left ({\eta _1,\chi _1}\right )\mathclose {} of \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma , the resulting type \mathopen {}\left ({\eta _0,\chi _0}\right )\mathclose {}\cong _{\mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma }\mathopen {}\left ({\eta _1,\chi _1}\right )\mathclose {} is definitionally equivalent to the following dependent sum:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {}\left ({g\colon B\to A,\epsilon \colon f\circ g =1_{B}}\right )\mathclose {} be a section of a function f\colon A\to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} P &:\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {},\epsilon \mathopen {}\left ({fx}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\,\mathopen {}\left ({fx}\right )\mathclose {}} \mathopen {}\left ({x,\mathsf {refl}}\right )\mathclose {} \\ P &\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({p : g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} = x}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({p : g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} = x}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({p : g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} = x}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({p : g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} = x}\right )\mathclose {}}} \epsilon \mathopen {}\left ({fx}\right )\mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {},\epsilon \mathopen {}\left ({fx}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}\cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\,\mathopen {}\left ({fx}\right )\mathclose {}}\mathopen {}\left ({x,\mathsf {refl}}\right )\mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {},\epsilon \mathopen {}\left ({fx}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}=_{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\,\mathopen {}\left ({fx}\right )\mathclose {}}\mathopen {}\left ({x,\mathsf {refl}}\right )\mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.
For any function f\colon A\to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

Without loss of generality, we may assume that f has an inverse \mathopen {}\left ({g,\epsilon ,\eta ,\chi }\right )\mathclose {}. By , it suffices to argue that the type of inverses to f is a retract of a proposition; the type of inverses to f is in fact equivalent to the type of sections equipped with a coherent remainder:

By , it suffices to show that the type of sections to f is a proposition and that for every such section, the coherent remainder thereof is a proposition. The latter is , and the former follows from by means of the retraction \mathopen {}\left ({g,\eta }\right )\mathclose {}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being an embedding is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: That a given function f\colon A \to B is an embedding is a proposition.
By and .
That a given map f\colon A \to B is bijective is a proposition.
By , via .
For any function f\colon A\to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

Without loss of generality, we may assume that f has an inverse \mathopen {}\left ({g,\epsilon ,\eta ,\chi }\right )\mathclose {}. By , it suffices to argue that the type of inverses to f is a retract of a proposition; the type of inverses to f is in fact equivalent to the type of sections equipped with a coherent remainder:

By , it suffices to show that the type of sections to f is a proposition and that for every such section, the coherent remainder thereof is a proposition. The latter is , and the former follows from by means of the retraction \mathopen {}\left ({g,\eta }\right )\mathclose {}.
Let f\colon A\to B be a retraction of some s\colon B\to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of -\circ f at 1_{A}.
The type of biinverses of a given function f\colon A\to B is a proposition.
Sections of sections and retractions of retractions are unique.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.

Let f\colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the postcomposition map \mathopen {}\left ({f\circ -}\right )\mathclose {}\colon \mathopen {}\left ({C\to A}\right )\mathclose {}\to \mathopen {}\left ({C\to B}\right )\mathclose {} is biinvertible.

The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.

A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r\colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({s:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({s:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({s:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({s:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}r\circ s \sim 1_{B}, as sectionhood witnesses form a right-cloven family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {}\left ({s,h}\right )\mathclose {} to \mathopen {}\left ({s',h'}\right )\mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {}\left ({\lambda {x}\mathpunct {.}\mathsf {act}_{r}\mathopen {}\left ({H_s x}\right )\mathclose {}\cdot h'x}\right )\mathclose {}.
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y\colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A biinverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with a biinverse as a biinvertible function.

Let s \colon B \to A and r \colon A \to B be a pair of maps. A homotopy h \colon r\circ s \sim 1_{B} exhibits s as a section of r and r as a retraction of s. Together, \mathopen {}\left ({s,r}\right )\mathclose {} are referred to as a section-retraction pair.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.
